During the panel, Raimi said, “Sometimes you need to destroy the original and move on. You have to move on while paying tribute to it, and nod to the audience while paving a new way, which is what Nicolas has done with this film.”






For many, it may feel like only yesterday that the Japanese horror craze had a foothold in the business of frightening audiences with raven-haired evil forces. Shockingly though, we are closing in on 20 years since the heyday of the original Japanese films and subsequent American remakes of The Ring and The Grudge. Now Sam Raimi (of The Evil Deadand classic Spider-Man movies) is back again with his Ghost House Pictures production company to produce an all-new The Grudge that will kick 2020 off in style. With Director Nicolas Pesce (The Eyes of My Mother, Piercing) and stars Andrea Risebourough, Lin Shaye, and GLOW's Betty Gilpin by his side, Raimi bought the first 

The first big takeaway is that we shouldn't be mistaken, this new film is not necessarily a reboot and it isn’t necessarily a sequel. Instead it is another tale from the same universe as the Japanese films, as well as the American ones. “The Grudge is an anthology series,” Pesce explains. “The movies are [about] different characters, different stories, different locations, different ghosts. Aside from the second and third American remakes, it’s all been very contained to Japan.”

While the characters from the original American remake were primarily foreigners living in and around Tokyo, this new version will take place wholly in the U.S. Pesce says, “The Grudge is like a virus; it’s super-contagious. So the fact that it would stay in the tiny city of Tokyo is unrealistic. It exists in Asia, Europe, Africa, in America, because it has spread. I know a lot of people think this is a remake of a remake, which is isn’t… this movie exists in the same world as the Japense movie, we’re just adding a chapter.”

I actually think this will be better than the previous iterations. The previous Americanized remake tried too hard to pay homage to the original without actually doing anything original itself. You don't do an American remake of a Japanese horror film and keep the locale and setting in Japan. Here's how it works. The Japanese originals create the overall plot and originality of the storyline; the Japanese are usually best at this (for supernatural ghost stories). The American studios then pay the original storywriters some hefty royalties (out of both respect and legal obligations) and do what the American studios do better - make the monsters, ghosts, and overall setting scarier or more cinematic (depending on the needs of the storyline). They usually have the creative freedom to move the storyline's central locale - something they failed to do in the first Americanized remake of Ju-On. One glance at this and I can already see this is light years ahead of the original American remake in the special effects department, and the original American remake was already light years ahead of Ju-On in that area. But this time they also look like they finally moved the setting to something more suited to American audiences (it looks like Washington, Minnesota, or maybe even Canada). Horror films pretty much all still suffer from the same problems - weak plot points in which the screenwriters overly rely on the stupidity of the characters in their story. That aspect won't change here. But it does appear to be something that will actually be an improvement over the original Americanized remake.
